nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated species documented on coral and rocky reef flats, protected shorelines, seaward reefs, and along drop-offs at depths of roughly 0–150 m. Has been reported as a benthic, solitary animal that typically rests with the head protruding from a crevice in the reef structure.

decorPreferences: very large, stable rockwork with caves, crevices, and tunnels large enough to conceal the entire body
rockwork securely anchored to the substrate — the species has been documented rearranging rock and sand
tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid with no gaps (documented escape artist)
long footprint over depth — at least ~240 cm length is generally advised for adult housing
PVC tubes or artificial caves may be used to supplement live rock if needed for hide structure

diet.notes: A piscivorous predator that has been documented feeding on fishes, cephalopods, and crustaceans in the wild. In captivity, generally accepts meaty marine foods such as whole fish, silversides, krill, shrimp, squid, and other crustacean flesh. Large meals every 3–4 days tend to be sufficient for adults. Feeding with tongs or a long feeding stick is strongly advised — the species has poor eyesight, relies heavily on smell, and has been known to bite hands by accident. Never hand-feed.

goodWith: very large, robust predatory tankmates not small enough to be swallowed
avoidWith: any fish small enough to be swallowed whole (will be eaten)
all crustaceans — shrimp, crabs, lobsters, hermit crabs (natural prey)
other moray eels in all but the largest exhibits
conspecifics (aggression toward similar species has been widely reported)

imageCandidates.notes: Bernard DUPONT; 4416x2649. Sharp in-situ shot showing head and long body section with the diagnostic yellow-on-dark speckled pattern and dark gill blotch, well-lit.
Alexander Vasenin; 2799x2099. Striking, vibrant sharp head/upper-body portrait from the Red Sea showing the yellow mottling and orange eye; different angle.
Bernard DUPONT; 4416x2649. Sharp shot of a different individual emerging from colorful reef, clear yellow speckling and gill blotch.

summary: The Yellow-Edged Moray (Gymnothorax flavimarginatus) is a large Indo-Pacific reef eel reaching roughly 2–2.4 m, distinguished by yellow margins on the dorsal and anal fins. Its adult size, predatory diet, and strong escape tendencies make it suitable only for very large, specialist aquaria.
careNotes:
The Yellow-Edged Moray is sometimes listed by retailers as an easy species to maintain day-to-day, but is most accurately considered expert-level because of its adult size, escape risk, and predatory diet. The eel itself tends to be hardy with respect to water quality, but housing a fully-grown 2–2.4 m specimen responsibly is well outside the scope of a typical home aquarium.

A tightly sealed, weighted or mechanically secured lid is generally considered essential — the species has been widely documented as an escape artist capable of lifting unweighted covers. Robust biological and mechanical filtration plus a strong protein skimmer are commonly advised given the heavy bioload that comes with large, meaty feedings. Rockwork should be securely anchored, as the eel has been reported to rearrange rocks and sand while moving through the tank.

Reef compatibility is generally described as 'with caution.' In practice, the species will consume any crustacean and any fish small enough to swallow, and its bulk can dislodge unsecured rockwork or corals. Adults reach 180–240 cm in length, and a tank footprint long enough to accommodate the animal comfortably — a minimum of around 240 cm in length — is widely advised; 1500 L is the most conservative and commonly cited responsible minimum for a fully-grown specimen.
breedingNotes: Reproduction in the wild is by external fertilization; aquarium reproduction is generally considered not achieved under home or even public-aquarium conditions for this species.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Product page: care level easy, temperament semi-aggressive, reef compatible 'with caution', 300 gal minimum, 72–78°F, SG 1.020–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, max size 96 in. Most active at night; tight lid required; never hand-feed.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, distribution, adult size, and depth/temperature range. Family Muraenidae, order Anguilliformes; max length 240 cm TL; depth 0–150 m; wild temperature 23.7–29°C (mean 27.8°C); Indo-Pacific from Red Sea/East Africa to French Polynesia, north to Hawaii and Ryukyus, south to New Caledonia, plus Eastern Pacific (Costa Rica, Panama, Galápagos); feeds on cephalopods, fishes, crustaceans; IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: ReefApp
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 1500 L minimum (largest recommendation), ~26°C, SG ~1.026, adult size up to 240 cm, reef-safe with caution, aggressive toward conspecifics, large meal every 3–4 days, escape specialist, rearranges rocks/sand.

additional.name: Reeflings
additional.notes: Cross-checked: 400 L minimum (low outlier — appears to describe juvenile housing), 22–28°C, pH 7.8–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, adult size up to 240 cm, territorial/best kept alone, not reef-safe, prefers live/frozen foods. Adult-size and water-parameter agreement strong; tank-size figure was discarded as juvenile-only.

fish.breedingNotes: No captive breeding documented in reviewed sources.
